blocks.text: because he is found by those who do not put him to the test, and manifests himself to those who do not distrust him.
For perverse thoughts separate people from God, and when his power is tested, it exposes the foolish;
because wisdom will not enter a deceitful soul, or dwell in a body enslaved to sin.
For a holy and disciplined spirit will flee from deceit, and will leave foolish thoughts behind, and will be ashamed at the approach of unrighteousness.

For wisdom is a kindly spirit, but will not free blasphemers from the guilt of their words; because God is witness of their inmost feelings, and a true observer of their hearts, and a hearer of their tongues.
Because the spirit of the Lord has filled the world, and that which holds all things together knows what is said,
7 : seems to be equated with .
therefore those who utter unrighteous things will not escape notice, and justice, when it punishes, will not pass them by.
For inquiry will be made into the counsels of the ungodly, and a report of their words will come to the Lord, to convict them of their lawless deeds;
because a jealous ear hears all things, and the sound of grumbling does not go unheard.
Beware then of useless grumbling, and keep your tongue from slander; because no secret word is without result, and a lying mouth destroys the soul.
11 : , the dangers of rash speech is a frequent topic in wisdom literature (e.g., Prov 2.12; 4.24; 10.11, 18–19, 31–32; Sir 9.18; 20.24–26; 28.12–26).
Do not invite death by the error of your life, or bring on destruction by the works of your hands;
12 : here is not simply the experience of mortality but rather a total annihilation and separation from God.
because God did not make death, and he does not delight in the death of the living.
For he created all things so that they might exist; the generative forces of the world are wholesome, and there is no destructive poison in them, and the dominion of Hades is not on earth.
14 : , both the abode of the dead in Greek tradition, and the god who presides over the underworld.
For righteousness is immortal.

1.1–15: Exhortation to love righteousness. A series of oppositions form the basis of making a choice for justice. On one side are righteousness, the Lord, God, wisdom, a holy and disciplined spirit, a kindly spirit, and the spirit of the Lord (vv. 1–7). On the other side are perverse thoughts, a deceitful soul, foolish thoughts, unrighteousness, blasphemers, and death (vv. 3–13). These oppositions are a version of the doctrine of the two ways (e.g., Ps 1).
But the ungodly by their words and deeds summoned death; considering him a friend, they pined away and made a covenant with him, because they are fit to belong to his company.
1.1–6.21: Exhortation to love righteousness. This opening section embodies a complex argument for justice in an elegant concentric structure:

1.16–2.24: The erroneous reasoning of the ungodly. In the style of the Greco-Roman diatribe, the author creates a fictional adversary, “the ungodly,” who embody the reasoning for injustice which the author will refute in subsequent texts. The section condenses arguments used to justify exploiting the weak. It apparently evokes the Epicurean insistence on materiality and enjoyment in life, but it is a fictional caricature of Epicurean thought, which also valued moderation.
1.16 : with death (lit., “him”; see v.14n.), cf. Isa 28.15.
